m_Item1.QuestionText:
What would be the output of the following?
public class MyClass {
    public static void main(String args[]) {
        int i=257;
        byte b=(byte)i;
        System.out.println("b= "+b);
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
A byte in Java is an 8-bit signed integer. Its range is from -128 to 127.
When you cast a larger data type (like int) to a smaller data type (like byte), Java truncates the higher-order bits.
Binary representation of 257 (as a 32-bit int):
00000000 00000000 00000001 00000001 (The 1 at the 9th bit position, and 1 at the 0th bit position)
Truncating to 8 bits (for byte):
The cast takes only the lowest 8 bits: 00000001
Interpreting the 8-bit value as a signed byte:
The 8-bit binary 00000001 represents the decimal value 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following program?
class OperatorsOutput {
    public static void main(String s[]) {
        int a = 12 + 21 * 3 - 9 / 2;
        int b = 14 - 32 * 4 + 175 / 8 - 3;
        if(++a > 71 && --b < 20) {
            System.out.println("a = " + a + " b = " + b);
        }
        if(b-- == -97 || a-- < 100) {
            System.out.println("a = " + a + " b = " + b);
        }
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
Inside main, 2 integer variables a, b are declared.
By following operator precedence,
a = 12 + 21 * 3 - 9 / 2 = 12 + 63 - 9 / 2 = 12 + 63 - 4 = 75 - 4 = 71
b = 14 - 32 * 4 + 175 / 8 - 3 = 14 - 128 + 175 / 8 - 3 = 14 - 128 + 21 - 3 = -114 + 21 - 3 = -93 - 3 = -96
Then first if condition is checked, ++a > 71 && --b < 20 is 72 > 71 && -97 < 20 = true && true = true
So, if is executed and value of a which is 72 and b which is -97 are printed.

Then next if condition is checked,
b-- == -97 || a-- < 100 is
-97 == -97 || a-- < 100 is
(true || anything(either true or false)) = true
(by short circuiting, as only first part(b-- == -97) of the or (||) condition suffices to evaluate the result, so the second part (a-- < 100) is not executed and hence a is not decremented)
So only b decrements and display statement is executed and prints value of a and b as 72 and -98 respectively.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following program?
public class Doubt {
    public static void main(String s[]) {
        int x = 20;
        int y = 25;
        if (++x < (y = y -= 4) || (x = x += 4) > y) {
            System.out.println(x + "," + y);
        }
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
Condition in if Statement:
++x < (y = y -= 4) || (x = x += 4) > y:
++x increments x first, so x becomes 21.
(y = y -= 4):
y -= 4 decrements y by 4, so y becomes 21.
The assignment y = y -= 4 assigns 21 to y.
Now the condition is 21 < 21 || (x = x += 4) > 21.
21 < 21 is false.
(x = x += 4):
x += 4 increments x by 4, so x becomes 25.
The assignment x = x += 4 assigns 25 to x.
25 > 21 is true.
Result of the if Statement:
Since false || true evaluates to true, the condition of the if statement is true.
Therefore, the code inside the if block executes:
System.out.println(x + "," + y); prints 25,21.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Compare the following programs ForSample1, ForSample2 and WhileSample
class ForSample1 {
    public static void main(String s[]) {
        for(int i = 0; i <= 5; i++ ) {
            System.out.println("i = " + i );
        }
    }
}

class ForSample2 {
    public static void main(String s[]) {
        int i = 0;
        for( ;i <= 5; ) {
            System.out.println("i = " + i );
            i++;
        }
    }
}

class WhileSample {
    public static void main(String s[]) {
        int i =0;
        while(i <= 5) {
            System.out.println("i = " + i );
            i++;
        }
    }
}

Options.OptionText: Output of all the three programs is same.
Output of ForSample1 and ForSample2 is same, but they are different from WhileSample.
Output of ForSample2 and WhileSample is same, but they are different from ForSample1.
ForSample2 does not compile since there is no code before first semicolon and no code after second semicolon.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Consider first program ForSample1. Execution of program starts from main. Inside main is a for loop.
Initialization Condition : i = 0
Terminating Condition : i <= 5
Number of Iterations : 6
Increment Value : 1(i++)
For every iteration, value of i is displayed. As loop terminates after i increments from 5 to 6, values from 0 to 5 are printed.
Consider second program ForSample2. Execution of program starts from main. Inside main, an integer variable i is declared and initialized to 0. Next is a for loop. As we have already initialized i, there need not be initialization condition.
Terminating Condition : i <= 5
Number of Iterations : 6
For every iteration, value of i is displayed and is then incremented. As loop terminates after i increments from 5 to 6, values from 0 to 5 are printed.
Consider third program WhileSample. Execution of program starts from main. Inside main, an integer variable i is declared and initialized to 0. Next is a while loop which is executed if condition i <= 5 is satisfied.
For every iteration, value of i is displayed and is then incremented. As loop terminates after i increments from 5 to 6, values from 0 to 5 are printed.
Hence all the programs give same output.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public class BitwiseNotOperator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int i = 50;
        System.out.print(~i);
        System.out.print(",");
        System.out.print(~--i);
        System.out.print(",");
        System.out.print(~++i);
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
First System.out.print(~i);:

This statement prints the bitwise NOT of i.
Binary representation of 50 is 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0011 0010.
Bitwise NOT of 50 flips all bits, resulting in -51 in two's complement representation.
Therefore, the output is -51.
Second System.out.print(~--i);:

--i decrements i to 49.
Binary representation of 49 is 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0011 0001.
Bitwise NOT of 49 flips all bits, resulting in -50 in two's complement representation.
Therefore, the output is -50.
Third System.out.print(~++i);:

++i increments i back to 50.
Binary representation of 50 is 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0011 0010.
Bitwise NOT of 50 flips all bits, resulting in -51 in two's complement representation.
Therefore, the output is -51.

m_Item1.Explanation:
b = (byte)(b << 1);
The << operator is the left shift operator, which shifts all bits of b to the left by one position. The binary representation of 5 is 00000101.
Shifting left by one position: 00000101 << 1 results in 00001010.
00001010 in binary is equal to 10 in decimal.
The result is cast back to a byte.

m_Item1.QuestionText:

System.out.print(b);

b = (byte)(b >> 1);
The >> operator is the arithmetic right shift operator, which shifts all bits of b to the right by one position, and the sign bit is used to fill the leftmost bits.
The binary representation of 5 is 00000101.
Shifting right by one position: 00000101 >> 1 results in 00000010.
00000010 in binary is equal to 2 in decimal.
The result is cast back to a byte.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a legal identifier in java ?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Rules for defining Java Identifiers
There are certain rules for defining a valid java identifier. These rules must be followed, otherwise we get compile-time error.
•The only allowed characters for identifiers are all alphanumeric characters([A-Z],[a-z],[0-9]), ‘$‘(dollar sign) and ‘_‘ (underscore).
•Identifiers should not start with digits([0-9]). For example “123geeks” is a not a valid java identifier.
•Java identifiers are case-sensitive.
•There is no limit on the length of the identifier but it is advisable to use an optimum length of 4 – 15 letters only.
•Reserved Words can’t be used as an identifier. For example “int while = 20;” is an invalid statement as while is a reserved word. There are 53 reserved words in Java.
